stem: In a legal outcomes study comparing Judge A vs. Judge B: Overall, Treatment Y has a higher acquittal rate (60.9% vs 53.3%). However, when stratified by case severity: In misdemeanors (n_X=40, n_Y=80): Treatment X achieves 76% vs Treatment Y's 70%. In felonies (n_X=80, n_Y=30): Treatment X achieves 42% vs Treatment Y's 36%. The true population proportion of misdemeanors is uncertain, estimated between 31% and 49%. Which treatment is actually better, and by how much?

ground_truth.answer: This is Simpson's Paradox. Treatment X is better in BOTH subgroups. The aggregate reversal occurs because X is disproportionately applied to felonies (harder cases). Adjusted difference (X - Y): [0.06, 0.065], point estimate: 0.06. Treatment X is genuinely better by 6.0 percentage points.

solution_steps: Aggregate rates: X=53.3%, Y=60.9% (Y appears better).
Stratified: In misdemeanors: X=76% > Y=70%. In felonies: X=42% > Y=36%.
X is better in BOTH subgroups — this is Simpson's Paradox.
The paradox arises because Judge A is applied more to felonies (lower baseline rate), skewing the aggregate.
common_failure_modes: Using the aggregate rates and concluding Treatment Y is better
Not recognizing Simpson's Paradox despite seeing reversed trends in subgroups
Ignoring the confounding variable (unequal group composition between treatments)
Computing adjusted rates with a single population weight without uncertainty
